item.title: Middle East Oil Markets Weaken on Optimism Over Increased Supply
item.description: Middle Eastern crude markets weakened as the US-Iran agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz spurred optimism around an increase in flows from the oil-rich region.

item.title: Evergrande Liquidators Seek Review of PwC HK’s $128 Million Deal
item.description: China Evergrande’s liquidators are seeking a judicial review of a HK$1 billion ($127.6 million) shareholder compensation deal a regulator struck with PricewaterhouseCoopers’ Hong Kong affiliate.

item.title: Global Funds Pile Into India Bonds as Oil Cools
item.description: Lower crude prices improves inflation outlook and eases pressure on current account deficit.

item.title: Chinese Bonds Draw Foreign Funds in May After a Year of Outflows
item.description: Foreign investors returned to Chinese sovereign bonds in May for the first time in over a year, drawn by the market’s resilience while a brutal selloff battered global debt.

item.title: Morgan Stanley Cuts Oil Forecasts as Hormuz Deal Revives Supply
item.description: Morgan Stanley made substantial cuts to its oil-price forecasts for the coming quarters as the interim deal between the US and Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz stands to revive regional output and boost supplies.

item.title: China’s Oil Refiners Slash Output After Crude Imports Plunge
item.description: Chinese oil refiners sharply reduced output last month to the weakest level in nearly four years, after the country’s crude imports plunged to an eight-year low due to the near-halt to shipments from the Persian Gulf.

item.title: Oil Slide Brings Little Relief to Hard-Hit Philippine Consumers
item.description: Oil’s much-awaited decline may offer little respite for hard-hit consumers in the Philippines, where local pump prices were quick to skyrocket but could take up to a year to normalize.

item.title: Japan Poised to Hike Interest Rates to Highest Since 1995
item.description: The Bank of Japan (BOJ) headquarters in Tokyo.

item.title: China’s First Consumer Spending Drop Since Covid Imperils Growth
item.description: China’s consumer spending contracted last month for the first time since the pandemic and investment deteriorated, exposing risks still facing the economy even as it benefits from booming exports and a deescalation in tensions around Iran.

item.title: NZ Economists Trim Inflation Forecasts, Still Expect Rate Hikes
item.description: New Zealand economists have trimmed their inflation forecasts after a report Tuesday that fuel prices and airfares retreated in May even before the impact of potential peace in the Middle East.

item.title: Jardine to Buy Back $500 Million Shares, Boost Returns
item.description: Jardine Matheson Holdings Ltd. will buy back $500 million of its own shares by the end of next year as the Hong Kong company lays out targets for its transformation from a long-term owner-operator into an active investor more akin to a private equity fund.

item.title: Hong Kong Stocks Face Pressure From $33 Billion Lockup Expiry
item.description: Hong Kong’s underperforming stock market may come under renewed pressure as a large amount of shares frozen under restrictions tied to equities offerings become tradable next month.

item.title: AllianzGI Trims Bullish Yuan Bet, Turns Neutral After Rally
item.description: Allianz Global Investors has reduced some of its bullish yuan position and turned neutral, taking profits after a rally that made the Chinese currency Asia’s best performer this year.

item.title: Woodside Energy’s Louisiana LNG President Abruptly Departs
item.description: The top executive at Woodside Energy Group Ltd.’s $17.5 billion liquefied natural gas project in Louisiana has departed, effective Monday, just over a year after she was hired, according to a company memo seen by Bloomberg.

item.title: Worst Indonesian Credit Volatility in Years Imperils Debt Boom
item.description: The highest volatility in rupiah corporate bonds in four years is threatening to dent record issuance by companies in Southeast Asia’s largest economy at a time when government policy swings have hurt international investor confidence in Indonesian assets.

item.title: SpaceX Investors at US Retail Brokers Got at Least One IPO Share
item.description: Customers at some of the largest retail brokerage firms in the US each received at least a single share in SpaceX’s $86.2 billion IPO, underscoring how the offering was designed to give individual investors a sizable role.

item.title: BOJ Policy Architect Uchida to Take Spotlight on Rates Guidance
item.description: Bank of Japan Deputy Governor Shinichi Uchida will enter the spotlight Tuesday as the central bank’s key communicator, tasked with explaining the BOJ’s latest decision and the future path of policy.

item.title: Trader Loses $1 Million on Cabo Verde’s Stunning World Cup Tie
item.description: A single trader on Polymarket lost nearly $1 million when Cabo Verde fought Spain to a stunning draw on Monday in one of the most unlikely outcomes in recent World Cup history, powered by a 40-year-old goalkeeper who left the pitch in tears.

item.title: Cash Squeeze Set to Lift Taiwan’s Yields Further, Analysts Say
item.description: Taiwan’s yields have scope to climb further as tight cash conditions in the local market and concerns over rising inflation dampen demand for the island’s debt, according to analysts.

item.title: Brazilian Prosecutors Reject Vorcaro Deal in Banco Master Case
item.description: Brazilian prosecutors rejected a cooperation agreement proposed by Daniel Vorcaro, the former banker at the center of a massive fraud investigation, according to a person familiar with the matter.

item.title: Gold Holds Gain as Trump Touts Reopening of Hormuz This Week
item.description: Gold held a gain after US President Donald Trump said the Strait of Hormuz could reopen on Friday, likely easing an energy and inflation shock that has roiled global markets.

item.title: IG4 Offers to Buy Raizen Debt in Quest for Majority Stake
item.description: Private equity firm IG4 Capital, fresh off a deal to take control of petrochemical giant Braskem SA, is now setting its sights on sugar and ethanol producer Raizen SA, another major Brazilian company in distress.

item.title: Canada to Restrict Use of Personal Data for Custom Prices
item.description: The Canadian government is proposing stricter privacy rules that would limit businesses from using personal data to charge consumers higher prices, while giving individuals more power over their own information.

item.title: Oil Holds Losses After Iran Deal Spurs Stock Rally: Markets Wrap
item.description: Oil held losses after tumbling the most in more than two weeks on Monday as the US and Iran agreed to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, easing concerns over energy supplies and fueling a global equity rally. Stocks in Asia were set for a mixed open.

item.title: Oil Steadies Near Lowest Since March on Outlook for Hormuz Deal
item.description: Oil held the biggest drop in more than two weeks on optimism that a deal between the US and Iran will reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: China Built the Last Steel Boom. India’s Will Be Different
item.description: The next big growth market will align closely with domestic spending on infrastructure.

item.title: Where the US, Israel and Iran Stand on a Deal to End Months of War
item.description: On today’s Big Take podcast, we examine the major hurdles facing a proposed US-Iran peace deal, including how Israel fits into the equation.

item.title: Bolivia Tells Investors FX Unification, IMF Deal Are Coming Soon
item.description: Bolivia told investors it is close to agreeing on a financing program with the International Monetary Fund, likely after introducing a floating exchange rate to end a dollar peg that has been in place for more than 15 years.

item.title: Goldman-Backed Go to Debut After Biggest Japan IPO This Year
item.description: Taxi-hailing app provider Go Inc.’s shares will start trading on the Tokyo Stock Exchange on Tuesday, testing investor sentiment after the nation’s largest initial public offering so far this year.

item.title: Blackstone’s Ancestry Hires Bank of America for $2 Billion Loan
item.description: Ancestry.com Inc. is weighing a $2.25 billion loan deal to refinance some of its existing debt with the help of Bank of America Corp., according to people with knowledge of the matter.
